headline: Educators are converging on orchestrated agent loops that prompt themselves, with security and identity layers emerging to contain the resulting multi-agent risk.
body: The recommended channels lead with a shift away from manual prompting toward continuous agentic loops: Cole Medin builds a TypeScript loop using Pi with database observability and an orchestrator spawning parallel workers [0], and Cyril Imhof frames the same move as harness engineering and AI loops around Claude Fable 5, including for regulated industries [3]. IBM Technology supplies the security counterweight, debating whether AI agents are ready to detect and rotate compromised passwords [2] and detailing Kagenti, which protects multi-agent systems through identity, delegation chains, and zero-trust principles [4]. Supporting demos cover open and local tooling: Voicebox as a local-first ElevenLabs alternative [1], Z AI's free GLM-5.2 coding agent Z Code [5], and Moonshot's open-weight Kimi K2.7 Code [9]. Newer creators map the broader pattern, with Jack Roberts laying out a seven-level Hermes agent progression [10] and Microsoft Scout operating proactively across Teams, email, and calendar [12].

cited_items.title: The Creators of Claude Code and OpenClaw don't Prompt Their Agents Anymore?!
